files.SKILL.md:
# Field Traps

A working list of moves that feel productive and quietly cost
the session. Each trap has a better move beside it.

---

**"I know roughly how this goes — I'll act on instinct."**

The current task holds the specifics. Read the environment's
own documentation before drawing on memory. Every session's
constants — identifiers, paths, formats, test names — must
come from what is open in front of you, not from what you recall.

---

**"I'll read a little more before committing."**

Once you can name the first safe action, take it. Reads past
that point add context without changing the move. A session
that ends on thorough notes and no artifact has failed its
actual job.

---

**"The prior record probably still holds."**

If a persistent notebook exists, read it first. Treat entries
as strong orientation — then check whether the live environment
confirms them before acting on them. When live evidence
contradicts a stored entry, live evidence wins; update the entry.

---

**"I'll check the result and move on."**

A success code means the call was accepted. Re-open the surface
you changed and confirm the new state directly. Unverified
completions become the next session's first confusion.

---

**"The record named a location — I'll still explore first."**

When a prior entry names where the fault was and where the fix
landed, go there immediately. Read just enough to confirm the
context still holds, then apply the fix. Broad re-investigation
after a specific pointer is wasted motion.

---

**"I'll clean up nearby things while I'm here."**

Scope creep creates verification debt. The task names exactly
what to change. Touch only that. The parts of the environment
that are not named in the task are not yours to improve.

---

**"I'll capture my reasoning in the record."**

A record is for the next session, not for this one. Write one
entry per class of problem: the category that will recur, the
exact name of the check or test that surfaced the failure as
the environment reported it, the root cause in one sentence,
and where the correction was applied. Write only after the fix
is confirmed — an unverified hypothesis misdirects the reader.

---

**"One more read might sharpen the diagnosis."**

Once the file and failure shape are named, produce the fix.
The diff should be as narrow as the bug. A correct diagnosis
that produces no artifact is not a correct diagnosis — it is
an incomplete session.
